question:
For each positive integer $n$, let $f(n)$ be the number of ways to
make $n!$ cents using an unordered collection of coins, each worth $k!$
cents for some $k$, $1 \leq k \leq n$. Prove that for some constant $C$,
independent of $n$,
\[
n^{n^2/2 - Cn} e^{-n^2/4} \leq f(n) \leq n^{n^2/2 + Cn}e^{-n^2/4}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
solution:
(Suggested by Oleg Golberg)
Assume $n \geq 2$, or else the problem
is trivially false.
Throughout this proof, any $C_i$ will be a positive constant whose exact
value is immaterial.
As in the proof of Stirling's approximation, we
estimate for any fixed $c \in \RR$,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n (i+c) \log i =
\frac{1}{2} n^2 \log n - \frac{1}{4} n^2 + O(n \log n)
\]
by comparing the sum to an integral. This gives
\begin{align*}
n^{n^2/2-C_1 n} e^{-n^2/4} &\leq 1^{1+c} 2^{2+c} \cdots n^{n+c} \\
&\leq n^{n^2/2+C_2 n} e^{-n^2/4}.
\end{align*}
We now interpret $f(n)$ as counting the number of $n$-tuples
$(a_1, \dots, a_n)$ of nonnegative integers such that
\[
a_1 1! + \cdots + a_n n! = n!.
\]
For an upper bound on $f(n)$, we use the inequalities
$0 \leq a_i \leq n!/i!$ to deduce that there are at most $n!/i! + 1 \leq 2(n!/i!)$
choices for $a_i$. Hence
\begin{align*}
f(n) &\leq 2^n \frac{n!}{1!} \cdots \frac{n!}{n!} \\
&= 2^n 2^1 3^2 \cdots n^{n-1} \\
&\leq n^{n^2/2+C_3 n} e^{-n^2/4}.
\end{align*}
For a lower bound on $f(n)$, we note that if $0 \leq a_i < (n-1)!/i!$
for $i=2,\dots,n-1$ and $a_n = 0$,
then $0 \leq a_2 2! + \cdots + a_n n! \leq n!$, so there is a unique
choice of $a_1$ to complete this to a solution of
$a_1 1! + \cdots + a_n n! = n!$. Hence
\begin{align*}
f(n) &\geq \frac{(n-1)!}{2!} \cdots \frac{(n-1)!}{(n-1)!} \\
&= 3^1 4^2 \cdots (n-1)^{n-3} \\
&\geq n^{n^2/2+C_4 n} e^{-n^2/4}.
\end{align*}

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
For each positive integer $indexsize$, let $wayscount(indexsize)$ be the number of ways to
make $indexsize!$ cents using an unordered collection of coins, each worth $iterindex!$
cents for some $iterindex$, $1 \leq iterindex \leq indexsize$. Prove that for some
constant $constcap$, independent of $indexsize$,
\[
indexsize^{indexsize^2/2 - constcap\, indexsize} e^{-indexsize^2/4} \leq
wayscount(indexsize) \leq indexsize^{indexsize^2/2 + constcap\, indexsize}e^{-indexsize^2/4}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
descriptive_long.solution:
(Suggested by Oleg Golberg)
Assume $indexsize \geq 2$, or else the problem
is trivially false.
Throughout this proof, any $constseqi$ will be a positive constant whose exact
value is immaterial.
As in the proof of Stirling's approximation, we
estimate for any fixed $offsetcon \in \RR$,
\[
\sum_{loopindex=1}^{indexsize} (loopindex+offsetcon) \log loopindex =
\frac{1}{2} indexsize^2 \log indexsize - \frac{1}{4} indexsize^2 + O(indexsize \log indexsize)
\]
by comparing the sum to an integral. This gives
\begin{align*}
indexsize^{indexsize^2/2-constone\, indexsize} e^{-indexsize^2/4} &\leq 1^{1+offsetcon} 2^{2+offsetcon} \cdots indexsize^{indexsize+offsetcon} \\
&\leq indexsize^{indexsize^2/2+consttwo\, indexsize} e^{-indexsize^2/4}.
\end{align*}
We now interpret $wayscount(indexsize)$ as counting the number of $indexsize$-tuples
$(coeffbase_1, \dots, coeffbase_{indexsize})$ of nonnegative integers such that
\[
coeffbase_1 1! + \cdots + coeffbase_{indexsize} indexsize! = indexsize!.
\]
For an upper bound on $wayscount(indexsize)$, we use the inequalities
$0 \leq coefficienti \leq indexsize!/loopindex!$ to deduce that there are at most $indexsize!/loopindex! + 1 \leq 2(indexsize!/loopindex!)$
choices for $coefficienti$. Hence
\begin{align*}
wayscount(indexsize) &\leq 2^{indexsize} \frac{indexsize!}{1!} \cdots \frac{indexsize!}{indexsize!} \\
&= 2^{indexsize} 2^1 3^2 \cdots indexsize^{indexsize-1} \\
&\leq indexsize^{indexsize^2/2+constthree\, indexsize} e^{-indexsize^2/4}.
\end{align*}
For a lower bound on $wayscount(indexsize)$, we note that if $0 \leq coefficienti < (indexsize-1)!/loopindex!$
for $loopindex=2,\dots,indexsize-1$ and $coefficienti_{indexsize} = 0$,
then $0 \leq coefficienti_2 2! + \cdots + coefficienti_{indexsize} indexsize! \leq indexsize!$, so there is a unique
choice of $coeffbase_1$ to complete this to a solution of
$coeffbase_1 1! + \cdots + coeffbase_{indexsize} indexsize! = indexsize!$. Hence
\begin{align*}
wayscount(indexsize) &\geq \frac{(indexsize-1)!}{2!} \cdots \frac{(indexsize-1)!}{(indexsize-1)!} \\
&= 3^1 4^2 \cdots (indexsize-1)^{indexsize-3} \\
&\geq indexsize^{indexsize^2/2+constfour\, indexsize} e^{-indexsize^2/4}.
\end{align*}

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For each positive integer $labyrinth$, let $afterimage(labyrinth)$ be the number of ways to
make $labyrinth!$ cents using an unordered collection of coins, each worth $trelliswork!$
cents for some $trelliswork$, $1 \leq trelliswork \leq labyrinth$. Prove that for some constant $pinecone$,
independent of $labyrinth$,
\[
labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2 - pinecone labyrinth} e^{-labyrinth^2/4} \leq afterimage(labyrinth) \leq labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2 + pinecone labyrinth}e^{-labyrinth^2/4}.
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
(Suggested by Oleg Golberg)
Assume $labyrinth \geq 2$, or else the problem
is trivially false.
Throughout this proof, any $houndfish_{chandelier}$ will be a positive constant whose exact
value is immaterial.
As in the proof of Stirling's approximation, we
estimate for any fixed $riverbank \in \RR$,
\[
\sum_{chandelier=1}^{labyrinth} (chandelier+riverbank) \log chandelier =
\frac{1}{2} labyrinth^2 \log labyrinth - \frac{1}{4} labyrinth^2 + O(labyrinth \log labyrinth)
\]
by comparing the sum to an integral. This gives
\begin{align*}
labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2-bluebonnet_1 labyrinth} e^{-labyrinth^2/4} &\leq 1^{1+riverbank} 2^{2+riverbank} \cdots labyrinth^{labyrinth+riverbank} \\
&\leq labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2+rattlesnake_2 labyrinth} e^{-labyrinth^2/4}.
\end{align*}
We now interpret $afterimage(labyrinth)$ as counting the number of $labyrinth$-tuples
$(nightshade_1, \dots, nightshade_{labyrinth})$ of nonnegative integers such that
\[
nightshade_1 1! + \cdots + nightshade_{labyrinth} labyrinth! = labyrinth!.
\]
For an upper bound on $afterimage(labyrinth)$, we use the inequalities
$0 \leq nightshade_{chandelier} \leq labyrinth!/chandelier!$ to deduce that there are at most $labyrinth!/chandelier! + 1 \leq 2(labyrinth!/chandelier!)$
choices for $nightshade_{chandelier}$. Hence
\begin{align*}
afterimage(labyrinth) &\leq 2^{labyrinth} \frac{labyrinth!}{1!} \cdots \frac{labyrinth!}{labyrinth!} \\
&= 2^{labyrinth} 2^1 3^2 \cdots labyrinth^{labyrinth-1} \\
&\leq labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2+dragonfly_3 labyrinth} e^{-labyrinth^2/4}.
\end{align*}
For a lower bound on $afterimage(labyrinth)$, we note that if $0 \leq nightshade_{chandelier} < (labyrinth-1)!/chandelier!$
for $chandelier=2,\dots,labyrinth-1$ and $nightshade_{labyrinth} = 0$,
then $0 \leq nightshade_2 2! + \cdots + nightshade_{labyrinth} labyrinth! \leq labyrinth!$, so there is a unique
choice of $nightshade_1$ to complete this to a solution of
$nightshade_1 1! + \cdots + nightshade_{labyrinth} labyrinth! = labyrinth!$. Hence
\begin{align*}
afterimage(labyrinth) &\geq \frac{(labyrinth-1)!}{2!} \cdots \frac{(labyrinth-1)!}{(labyrinth-1)!} \\
&= 3^1 4^2 \cdots (labyrinth-1)^{labyrinth-3} \\
&\geq labyrinth^{labyrinth^2/2+bottlenose_4 labyrinth} e^{-labyrinth^2/4}.
\end{align*}

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For each positive integer $constant$, let $disorder(constant)$ be the number of ways to
make $constant!$ cents using an unordered collection of coins, each worth $immobile!$
cents for some $immobile$, $1 \leq immobile \leq constant$. Prove that for some constant $variable$,
independent of $constant$,
\[
constant^{constant^2/2 - variable\,constant} e^{-constant^2/4} \leq disorder(constant) \leq constant^{constant^2/2 + variable\,constant}e^{-constant^2/4}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
(Suggested by Oleg Golberg)
Assume $constant \geq 2$, or else the problem
is trivially false.
Throughout this proof, any $fluctuating$ will be a positive constant whose exact
value is immaterial.
As in the proof of Stirling's approximation, we
estimate for any fixed $changeful \in \RR$,
\[
\sum_{terminal=1}^{constant} (terminal+changeful) \log terminal =
\frac{1}{2} constant^2 \log constant - \frac{1}{4} constant^2 + O(constant \log constant)
\]
by comparing the sum to an integral. This gives
\begin{align*}
constant^{constant^2/2-unsteady\,constant} e^{-constant^2/4} &\leq 1^{1+changeful} 2^{2+changeful} \cdots constant^{constant+changeful} \\
&\leq constant^{constant^2/2+unstable\,constant} e^{-constant^2/4}.
\end{align*}
We now interpret $disorder(constant)$ as counting the number of $constant$-tuples
$(stagnant_1, \dots, stagnant_{constant})$ of nonnegative integers such that
\[
stagnant_1 1! + \cdots + stagnant_{constant} constant! = constant!.
\]
For an upper bound on $disorder(constant)$, we use the inequalities
$0 \leq stagnant_{terminal} \leq constant!/terminal!$ to deduce that there are at most $constant!/terminal! + 1 \leq 2(constant!/terminal!)$
choices for $stagnant_{terminal}$. Hence
\begin{align*}
disorder(constant) &\leq 2^{constant} \frac{constant!}{1!} \cdots \frac{constant!}{constant!} \\
&= 2^{constant} 2^1 3^2 \cdots constant^{constant-1} \\
&\leq constant^{constant^2/2+wavering\,constant} e^{-constant^2/4}.
\end{align*}
For a lower bound on $disorder(constant)$, we note that if $0 \leq stagnant_{terminal} < (constant-1)!/terminal!$
for $terminal=2,\dots,constant-1$ and $stagnant_{constant} = 0$,
then $0 \leq stagnant_{2} 2! + \cdots + stagnant_{constant} constant! \leq constant!$, so there is a unique
choice of $stagnant_{1}$ to complete this to a solution of
$stagnant_1 1! + \cdots + stagnant_{constant} constant! = constant!$. Hence
\begin{align*}
disorder(constant) &\geq \frac{(constant-1)!}{2!} \cdots \frac{(constant-1)!}{(constant-1)!} \\
&= 3^1 4^2 \cdots (constant-1)^{constant-3} \\
&\geq constant^{constant^2/2+vacillate\,constant} e^{-constant^2/4}.
\end{align*}

garbled_string.question:
For each positive integer $qzxwvtnp$, let $rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp)$ be the number of ways to
make $qzxwvtnp!$ cents using an unordered collection of coins, each worth $hjgrksla!$
cents for some $hjgrksla$, $1 \leq hjgrksla \leq qzxwvtnp$. Prove that for some constant $qazwsxed$,
independent of $qzxwvtnp$,
\[
qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2 - qazwsxed qzxwvtnp} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4} \leq rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp) \leq qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2 + qazwsxed qzxwvtnp}e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
(Suggested by Oleg Golberg)
Assume $qzxwvtnp \geq 2$, or else the problem
is trivially false.
Throughout this proof, any $edcvfrtg$ will be a positive constant whose exact
value is immaterial.
As in the proof of Stirling's approximation, we
estimate for any fixed $mnbvcxzq \in \RR$,
\[
\sum_{abcdefgh=1}^{qzxwvtnp} (abcdefgh+mnbvcxzq) \log abcdefgh =
\frac{1}{2} qzxwvtnp^2 \log qzxwvtnp - \frac{1}{4} qzxwvtnp^2 + O(qzxwvtnp \log qzxwvtnp)
\]
by comparing the sum to an integral. This gives
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2-yhnujmik qzxwvtnp} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4} &\leq 1^{1+mnbvcxzq} 2^{2+mnbvcxzq} \cdots qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp+mnbvcxzq} \\
&\leq qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2+ikmjunhy qzxwvtnp} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4}.
\end{align*}
We now interpret $rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp)$ as counting the number of $qzxwvtnp$-tuples
$(uvwxyzzz_1, \dots, uvwxyzzz_{qzxwvtnp})$ of nonnegative integers such that
\[
uvwxyzzz_1 1! + \cdots + uvwxyzzz_{qzxwvtnp} qzxwvtnp! = qzxwvtnp!.
\]
For an upper bound on $rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp)$, we use the inequalities
$0 \leq plmoknij \leq qzxwvtnp!/abcdefgh!$ to deduce that there are at most $qzxwvtnp!/abcdefgh! + 1 \leq 2(qzxwvtnp!/abcdefgh!)$
choices for $plmoknij$. Hence
\begin{align*}
rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp) &\leq 2^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{qzxwvtnp!}{1!} \cdots \frac{qzxwvtnp!}{qzxwvtnp!} \\
&= 2^{qzxwvtnp} 2^1 3^2 \cdots qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp-1} \\
&\leq qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2+olpazsxw qzxwvtnp} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4}.
\end{align*}
For a lower bound on $rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp)$, we note that if $0 \leq plmoknij < (qzxwvtnp-1)!/abcdefgh!$
for $abcdefgh=2,\dots,qzxwvtnp-1$ and $uvwxyzzz_{qzxwvtnp} = 0$,
then $0 \leq uvwxyzzz_2 2! + \cdots + uvwxyzzz_{qzxwvtnp} qzxwvtnp! \leq qzxwvtnp!$, so there is a unique
choice of $uvwxyzzz_1$ to complete this to a solution of
$uvwxyzzz_1 1! + \cdots + uvwxyzzz_{qzxwvtnp} qzxwvtnp! = qzxwvtnp!$. Hence
\begin{align*}
rtyuipas(qzxwvtnp) &\geq \frac{(qzxwvtnp-1)!}{2!} \cdots \frac{(qzxwvtnp-1)!}{(qzxwvtnp-1)!} \\
&= 3^1 4^2 \cdots (qzxwvtnp-1)^{qzxwvtnp-3} \\
&\geq qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp^2/2+wsxedcrf qzxwvtnp} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/4}.
\end{align*}

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  5 be an integer and put

g(n) = #{ unordered multisets {k1!, k2!, \ldots  } \subset  {1!, 2!, \ldots  , n!} having total value n!/2 } .

(The quantity n!/2 is an integer for every n \geq  2.)

Prove that there is an absolute constant C > 0 such that, for every integer n \geq  5,

        n^{n^{2}/2 - C n}
   e^{ - n^{2}/4 }   \leq   g(n)  \leq   n^{n^{2}/2 + C n}
   e^{ - n^{2}/4 } .
kernel_variant.solution:
Fix an integer n \geq  5 and denote
        N := n!/2 .
Every unordered multiset of factorial-coins whose total value is N can be encoded
by an n-tuple of non-negative integers
        a = (a1, \ldots  , an)
satisfying the linear diophantine equation
        a1\cdot 1! + a2\cdot 2! + \ldots  + an\cdot n! = N.                (\star )
In what follows we repeatedly use the classical asymptotic estimate, valid for
any fixed real constant c,
        \Sigma _{j=1}^{n} (j + c) log j = \frac{1}{2} n^2 log n - \frac{1}{4} n^2 + O(n log n).      (1)
(The proof is a standard comparison of the sum with an integral and is the
core of Stirling's formula.)

---------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Upper bound for g(n).
---------------------------------------------------------------------
For each i we have 0 \leq  ai \leq  \lfloor N/i!\rfloor , so the number of possible values of ai is
        \lfloor N/i!\rfloor  + 1 \leq  3 N/i!         (1 \leq  i \leq  n).                (2)
Indeed, for i \leq  n-1 we have N/i! \geq  1, hence \lfloor x\rfloor  + 1 \leq  2x \leq  3x; for i = n we
have N/n! = \frac{1}{2}, so \lfloor \frac{1}{2}\rfloor  + 1 = 1 \leq  3\cdot \frac{1}{2} as well.  By (2)
        g(n) \leq  \prod _{i=1}^n 3 N / i!
              = 3^n \cdot  \prod _{i=1}^n N/i! .                        (3)
Because N = n!/2 we may factor out 2^{-n}:
        \prod _{i=1}^n N/i! = 2^{-n} \prod _{i=1}^n n!/i! .            (4)
A standard telescoping cancellation (or induction) shows that
        \prod _{i=1}^n n!/i! = \prod _{j=2}^n j^{j-1}.                 (5)
Substituting (4)-(5) into (3) gives
        g(n) \leq  (3/2)^n \cdot  \prod _{j=2}^n j^{j-1}.                 (6)
Taking logarithms and applying (1) with c = -1 we obtain
        log g(n) \leq  n log(3/2)
                    + \Sigma _{j=2}^n (j-1) log j
                  = \frac{1}{2} n^2 log n - \frac{1}{4} n^2 + O(n log n).
The additive term n log(3/2) is absorbed in the O(n log n) remainder.  After
exponentiation we find an absolute constant C_1 such that
        g(n) \leq  n^{n^2/2 + C_1 n} e^{-n^2/4}.                 (7)

---------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Lower bound for g(n).
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Set a1 = an = 0 and allow the variables a3, \ldots  , a_{n-1} to vary
independently in the ranges
        0 \leq  ai \leq  \lfloor  (n-2)! / i! \rfloor     (3 \leq  i \leq  n-1).          (8)
Let
        S := \Sigma _{i=3}^{n-1} ai i! .
Since each summand in (8) is at most (n-2)! and there are n-3 of them,
        S \leq  (n-3)(n-2)!  <  N = n!/2                    (n \geq  5).
Consequently the difference R := N - S is a non-negative even integer.
Define
        a2 := R/2 .
Because R \leq  N we have a2 \leq  N/2! = n!/4, so (a1, \ldots  , an) meets the requirement
(\star ).  Different choices in (8) yield distinct solutions; hence
        g(n) \geq  \prod _{i=3}^{n-1} ( \lfloor  (n-2)! / i! \rfloor  + 1 )
              \geq  \prod _{i=3}^{n-1} ( (n-2)! / i! )                (9)
              =          (n-2)!^{n-3}
                 ------------------------------------------------
                 3!\cdot 4!\cdot \ldots \cdot (n-1)! .
Writing the factorials in the denominator multiplicatively and isolating the
factor i = n-1 gives
        g(n) \geq  [ \prod _{t=4}^{n-2} t^{t-3} ] / (n-1).           (10)
Taking logarithms and using (1) with c = -3,
        log g(n) \geq  \Sigma _{t=4}^{n-2} (t-3) log t - log(n-1)
                  = \frac{1}{2} n^2 log n - \frac{1}{4} n^2 + O(n log n).
The final term -log(n-1) is of lower order and can be absorbed. Therefore
there exists an absolute constant C_2 with
        g(n) \geq  n^{n^2/2 - C_2 n} e^{-n^2/4}.                 (11)

---------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conclusion.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Combining (7) and (11) and enlarging the larger of C_1, C_2 if necessary we find
a universal constant C such that, for every n \geq  5,
        n^{n^{2}/2 - C n} e^{-n^{2}/4} \leq  g(n) \leq  n^{n^{2}/2 + C n} e^{-n^{2}/4} .
This completes the proof.

core_steps: Encode a coin selection as an n-tuple (a1,…,an) of non-negative integers satisfying Σ ai·i! = n!
Crude upper bound: each ai ∈ [0, n!/i!] ⇒ #(choices) ≤ K·(n!/i!) for a fixed K>1, so f(n) ≤ ∏ K(n!/i!)
Crude lower bound: restrict a2,…,a_{n−1} to a small rectangular box (e.g. ai < (n−1)!/i!), set an=0, then the equation forces a1; thus f(n) ≥ ∏ L((n−1)!/i!) for some L>0
Rewrite the products as 1^{1+c}·2^{2+c}·…·n^{n+c} and approximate ∑(i+c)log i by an integral (Stirling-type) to obtain n^{n²/2±Cn}e^{−n²/4}

slot1.description: constant used to turn (n!/i!) + 1 into a multiple of n!/i! in the upper bound

slot2.description: size of the rectangular box picked for the lower bound (factorial one step smaller than n!)

slot3.description: fixed shift in the exponent pattern 1^{1+c}·2^{2+c}·…·n^{n+c} employed in the Stirling estimate
slot3.original: c (any fixed real number, often taken as 0)

slot4.description: pre-setting the last coordinate in the lower-bound construction
